title: Earning Service Revenue — Cash vs On Account
event: Tran Consulting completes a $2,400 consulting job for a client. Prepare the entry.

entries.label: Earn it and collect it same day

lines.label: collected now
lines.trap: The easy case — but notice the credit is identical to the on-account case.

lines.label: work performed
lines.why: The work is DONE — that's what earns the revenue.
lines.trap: Revenue keys off the WORK, not the cash. The next variant proves it.

lines.label: claim on client
lines.why: The client's promise to pay is an asset.
lines.trap: Revenue does NOT wait for the cash. Done work = earned revenue, full stop.

lines.label: work performed
lines.why: Same credit as the cash case — earning is about the WORK, not the payment.
lines.trap: If you credited Unearned Revenue here, you flipped the timing: unearned is for cash BEFORE work.

memorize.body: Revenue is recognized when EARNED (work performed), not when cash shows up. Timing only changes the debit: Cash vs Accounts Receivable.
Cash before work → Unearned Revenue. Work before cash → Accounts Receivable. Map the timing before touching accounts.

questions.prompt: Tran finishes the $2,400 job but won't be paid for 30 days. Revenue recognized TODAY = ?

distractors.feedback: Accrual accounting doesn't wait for cash. The work is complete, so all $2,400 is earned today — the receivable holds the claim.

build.scaffold: Credit is always Service Revenue once the work is done. The debit just tracks HOW you'll be paid.
